does: This paper surveys expert opinion on AGI safety and governance practices by administering a 50-statement questionnaire to 51 leading experts from AGI labs, academia, and civil society organizations. The researchers assess agreement levels with proposed safety measures and governance approaches for systems approaching or potentially exceeding human-level performance across broad cognitive domains. Key findings reveal near-unanimous consensus on multiple safety practices: 98% of respondents agree that AGI labs should implement pre-deployment risk assessments, dangerous capabilities evaluations, third-party audits, usage restrictions, and red teaming. The survey identifies areas of strong agreement on best practices while likely revealing nuances in expert opinion about implementation details, prioritization, and emerging governance challenges in the AGI development landscape.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of research addressing safe AGI development and the emerging field of AI governance. It extends prior work documenting safety concerns in large language models and frontier AI systems by systematically gathering expert consensus on mitigation strategies. The survey methodology draws from longstanding practices in science and technology policy assessment while responding to recent calls within the AI safety community for identifying concrete best practices. It connects to institutional efforts by companies like OpenAI, DeepMind, and Anthropic that have begun implementing safety measures, as well as academic research on capability evaluations, interpretability, and alignment. The work also engages with broader governance literature examining how emerging technologies can be responsibly developed and deployed at scale.
enables: This expert consensus baseline enables future research to identify best-practice standards for AGI development that could inform industry self-regulation, policy proposals, and governance frameworks. The high levels of agreement provide normative grounding for organizations seeking to implement rigorous safety protocols and audit requirements. The survey findings can support arguments for standardized safety practices across AGI labs and inform discussions with policymakers developing regulatory approaches to frontier AI. The work points toward developing measurement and enforcement mechanisms for these agreed-upon practices, investigating implementation barriers, and understanding remaining disagreements on more contentious governance questions. It also enables comparative analysis as the field evolves, allowing researchers to track how expert opinion shifts as AGI capabilities advance and real-world deployment decisions become more pressing.
